method: Outside-in crawl (browser UA; www.nypl.org sits behind Imperva Incapsula, so headers are edge-obscured). Probed NYPL's OWN public APIs directly: the Digital Collections API at api.repo.nypl.org/api/v2 (token-authenticated — search returns HTTP 401 'HTTP Basic: Access denied' without a registered token; served by Ruby on Rails + Phusion Passenger + nginx), and the open, hypermedia Locations API at refinery.nypl.org/api/nypl/locations/v1.0/locations (PHP Slim — NYPL\Refinery\Server — returning branches, hours, amenities, access, and links to events/exhibitions/alerts). Enumerated the github.com/NYPL open-source org (discovery-api, nypl-design-system, web-reader, nypl-core, on-site-hold-request-service, patron-eligibility-service, nypl-library-card-app, ruby-sierra-api-client, and dozens more). Verified the NYC Open Data agency label 'New York Public Library (NYPL)' via the Socrata Discovery API: only 6 assets, mostly stale 2010-2011 branch-services stats plus a facilities layer.
headline_findings: NYPL is the counter-example of this project: an independent nonprofit (serving Manhattan, the Bronx, and Staten Island) that has already built and operated genuinely good PUBLIC APIs for years — where nearly every city agency traps its data in HTML or a vendor CRM.

The Digital Collections API (api.repo.nypl.org/api/v2) is a real, token-authenticated public API over millions of digitized items — items/search, items/{uuid}, MODS metadata, captures, rights, and collections; built on Ruby on Rails.

The Locations API (refinery.nypl.org/api/nypl/locations/v1.0) is an OPEN, hypermedia (HAL) API publishing every branch with hours, amenities, accessibility, and links to events, exhibitions, and alerts; built on PHP Slim.

NYPL open-sources nearly its entire stack at github.com/NYPL — the Research Catalog discovery-api, an accessibility-first React design system (nypl-design-system), the nypl-core ontology, a web-based ebook reader, and patron/hold/Sierra microservices. Ownership is exemplary.

NYC Open Data holds only 6 thin, mostly-stale NYPL datasets (2010-2011 branch-services stats + a facilities layer). NYPL's living data is on its own APIs, not NYC Open Data — a real finding, and the inverse of every other domain.

The remaining gaps are unification and one write: the three good APIs are separate contracts with different auth/hosts/shapes, and placing a HOLD (reservation) on a book — though backed by real internal services (on-site-hold-request-service, patron-eligibility-service, nypl-hold-request-consumer) — has no clean, documented, agent-native public API. See apis-observed.md and tech-stack.md.

apis_observed.note: Digital Collections API. items/search, items/{uuid}, items/mods/{uuid}, items/rights/{uuid}, collections, collections/{uuid}. Returns 401 'HTTP Basic: Access denied' without a registered token. Ruby on Rails + Phusion Passenger + nginx. A real, mature NYPL-owned API.

Locations API. locations, locations/{id}, plus HAL _links to /events, /exhibitions, /alerts, /amenities, /blogs. Rich payload: hours_data, geolocation, access, contacts, fundraising. PHP Slim (NYPL\Refinery\Server). Fully open.
The primary API supporting the NYPL Research Catalog. Node/AWS. Backed by the Sierra ILS (ruby-sierra-api-client) and a stream of patron/hold microservices.

nypl-design-system (accessibility-first React, 80 stars), web-reader (ebook reader), nypl-core (ontology), on-site-hold-request-service, nypl-hold-request-consumer, patron-eligibility-service, dgx-patron-creator-service, nypl-library-card-app, barcode-service. Exemplary open ownership.
Patron-facing site + Research Catalog UI. Edge-obscured by Incapsula; front end uses the NYPL design system. Where patrons place holds today, inside an authenticated account.
Only 6 NYPL datasets, mostly stale 2010-2011 branch-services stats + a facilities layer. Not where NYPL's real data lives.

fruit.name: Digital Collections (digitized items)
fruit.notes: Real, token-authenticated public API (api.repo.nypl.org/api/v2): items/search, items/{uuid}, MODS, captures, rights, collections. The flagship example of NYPL already doing the right thing. Gap is only agent-native packaging.

fruit.name: Digital Collections collections
fruit.notes: Curated groupings of digitized items, keyed on UUID, exposed through the same API.

fruit.name: Branches, hours & amenities
fruit.notes: Open, hypermedia Locations API (refinery.nypl.org) with hours_data, geolocation, access, amenities, and links to events/exhibitions/alerts. Far richer than the two stale Open Data facilities layers.

fruit.name: Events, classes & exhibitions
fruit.notes: Programs and exhibitions surfaced via the Locations API /events HAL link and the nypl.org calendar. Reconciled here as a first-class resource.

fruit.name: Research & circulating catalog
fruit.notes: Bibliographic search over research and circulating holdings; open-source discovery-api backed by the Sierra ILS. Availability/holdability drives whether a hold can be placed.

fruit.name: Branch-services statistics (historical)
fruit.notes: Circulation, attendance, program counts, and reference transactions by Adult/Young Adult/Juvenile — but STALE (2010-2011) and never updated. Boroughs present (Manhattan, Bronx, Staten Island) confirm NYPL's footprint vs. BPL/QPL.

fruit.name: Place a hold (reservation)
fruit.notes: The everyday resident transaction and the net-new WRITE surface. NYPL runs the internal pieces (on-site-hold-request-service, nypl-hold-request-consumer, patron-eligibility-service, ruby-sierra-api-client) and patrons place holds inside their authenticated account — but there is NO clean, documented, agent-native public hold API. This is the one real gap.

fruit.name: Library card application
fruit.notes: Applying for a library card is a real open-source app (nypl-library-card-app) backed by dgx-patron-creator-service — but is a UI workflow, not a documented public write API. An alternative net-new write surface; already partially exists.

meta.platform: NYPL-owned open-source stack — Ruby on Rails Digital Collections API (api.repo.nypl.org), PHP Slim Locations API (refinery.nypl.org), Node/AWS discovery-api + Sierra ILS, accessibility-first React design system; site behind Imperva Incapsula
meta.netnew: Hold (BookHold) — place and track a reservation on a catalog item via a clean, agent-native public API (backed by NYPL's existing internal hold services)
meta.tagline: NYPL is the counter-example: an independent nonprofit that already built and open-sourced genuinely good public APIs for its collections, locations, and catalog.
meta.headline: Where city agencies trap data in HTML or a vendor CRM, NYPL leads with real public APIs — the modernization work is to unify its three good APIs into one agent-native contract and close the single gap: placing a hold.

scorecard_notes.platform: Modern, largely self-built and open-sourced stack (Rails, PHP Slim, Node/AWS microservices, Sierra ILS, React design system) — not a legacy CMS or rented SaaS.
scorecard_notes.siteApi: Two real public APIs — Digital Collections (token) and the open hypermedia Locations API — plus the open-source Research Catalog discovery-api. The strongest siteApi of any domain assessed.
scorecard_notes.openData: Only 6 thin, mostly-stale NYPL datasets on NYC Open Data (2010-2011 branch stats + a facilities layer); NYPL's real data is on its own APIs, not Open Data.
scorecard_notes.coreData: Core objects — digital items, collections, locations, catalog bibs — are all fully available through NYPL-owned APIs.
scorecard_notes.writeApi: Holds and patron creation run on real internal services and UI workflows (library-card-app), but there is no clean, documented, agent-native public write API for placing a hold.
scorecard_notes.agent: No MCP or agent-native surface today; but the mature underlying APIs make the OpenAPI + MCP artifact here a light lift rather than a rebuild.
scorecard_notes.ownership: Exemplary — NYPL owns and open-sources nearly its entire stack (github.com/NYPL), including the nypl-core ontology; no vendor lock on its core data.
